coredata.dc:title: Individual and simultaneous electrochemical determination of metanil yellow and curcumin on carbon quantum dots based glassy carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Shereema, Rayammarakkar M.
Rao, Talasila P.
Sameer Kumar, V.B.
Sruthi, T.V.
Vishnu, R.
Prabhu, G.R.D.
Sharath Shankar, S.
coredata.dc:description: Adulteration of medicinally valuable curcumin (CU) with harmful chemicals as metanil yellow (MY) in recent years have demanded for quick detection techniques of the adulterants. The voltammetric behavior of CU and MY on bare glassy carbon electrode (BGCE) and carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs/GCE) was studied by both cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in phosphate buffer solution of pH 5.4. The MY responded to the CQDs/GCE with two anodic peaks at −0.004 V and 0.136 V and two cathodic peaks at −0.112 and −0.048 V. Under similar conditions CU exhibited two anodic peaks at 0.28 V and 0.55 V and one cathodic peak at 0.25 V. The overlapped voltammogram obtained for CU and MY on BGCE was well separated on the CQDs/GCE. The interference studies revealed that the compounds, demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin, which are commonly associated with CU, did not interfere with the detection of MY. Real sample was analyzed with fabricated electrode and the recovery values >98% were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metanil yellow
Curcumin
Cyclic voltammetry
Differential pulse voltammetry
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of MY on CQDs/GCE
Effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic oxidation of MY at CQDs/GCE
Effect of MY concentration and solution pH on CQDs/GCE
Electrochemical behavior of CU on CQDs/GCE
Effect of solution pH and concentration of CU
Simultaneous analysis of MY and CU
Determination of MY and CU in turmeric powder samples by CQDs/GCE

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Department of Science and Technology, Government of West Bengal

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ms. Shereema R. M. and Sharath Shankar are thankful to CSIR , New Delhi, India for the award of Senior Research Fellowship and Nehru Postdoctoral Fellowship respectively. Vishnu R acknowledges DST, Govt. of India for Inspire fellowships.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms of BGCE (curve a), CQDs/GCE (curve b) in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 in 0.1 M KCl with a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
Cyclic voltammograms of 2 μM MY on BGCE and CQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.4.
(a): Cyclic voltammograms at the CQDs/GCE in the presence of different concentrations (0.2 μΜ to 400 μΜ) of MY (b) Plot of Ip vs. log [MY] plots.
(a): DPV of MY with different concentrations (0.06 to 40 μM) on CQDs/GCE in pH 5.4 PBS (b) Plot of Ip vs. log [MY] plots.
Cyclic voltammograms of 2 μM CU in PBS of pH 5.4 on BGCE (curve and CQDs/GCE (curve b) with a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
(a): Cyclic voltammograms of different concentrations of CU (0.8 to 100 μM) at the CQDs/GCE (b) Plot of Ip vs. log [CU] plots.
(a): DPV of CU with different concentration (0.4 to 200 μM) on CQDs/GCE in pH 5.4 PBS (b) Plot of Ip vs. log [CU] plots.
(a): DPV of different concentration of MY (0.06 to 50 μM) in presence of 10 μM CU (b) DPV of different concentration of CU (0.4 to 10 μM) in presence of 5 μM MY at CQDs/GCE in pH 5.4 PBS.
(a): Selectivity studies of MY and (b) CU in presence of demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin.

head.ce:title: Individual and simultaneous electrochemical determination of metanil yellow and curcumin on carbon quantum dots based glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Sciences & Technology Division (CSTD), CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science & Technology (CSIR-NIIST), Trivandrum 695019, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science & Technology (CSIR-NIIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), New Delhi 110001, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, Central University of Kerala, Nileshwar, Kasargod, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agroprocessing and Natural Products Division (ANPD), CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (CSIR-NIIST), Trivandrum 695019, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-National Institute for Interdisciplinary Science and Technology (CSIR-NIIST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adulteration of medicinally valuable curcumin (CU) with harmful chemicals as metanil yellow (MY) in recent years have demanded for quick detection techniques of the adulterants. The voltammetric behavior of CU and MY on bare glassy carbon electrode (BGCE) and carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs/GCE) was studied by both cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in phosphate buffer solution of pH 5.4. The MY responded to the CQDs/GCE with two anodic peaks at −0.004 V and 0.136 V and two cathodic peaks at −0.112 and −0.048 V. Under similar conditions CU exhibited two anodic peaks at 0.28 V and 0.55 V and one cathodic peak at 0.25 V. The overlapped voltammogram obtained for CU and MY on BGCE was well separated on the CQDs/GCE. The interference studies revealed that the compounds, demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin, which are commonly associated with CU, did not interfere with the detection of MY. Real sample was analyzed with fabricated electrode and the recovery values >98% were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: The polyphenol curcumin (1,7-bis (1,7-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadione-3,5-dione) is an important natural phytochemical found in the rhizomes of Curcuma longa or turmeric, which is known for its medicinal properties. Due to its high medicinal potential with virtually no side effects, this compound has attracted considerable interest in recent years [1–3]. Curcumin has been shown to possess antioxidant, antitumor activities, antiviral, antibacterial, antifungal, immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory [4–6]. It has also been reported that curcumin regulates classic and alternative pathways in the nervous system, and is being used in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease, multiple sclerosis, and dementia [7–9]. It is yellow colored common food additive used as both coloring agent and spice. Currently, adulteration of curcumin with various non-permitted dyes including, sudan and matanil yellow is common in countries of Asia for economic gain. Metanil yellow (sodium salt of m- [(p-anilinophenyl) azo] benzene sulphonic acid) is a highly toxic and mutagenic monoazo dye. Their excess utilization, beyond the safe limits, in colored food can cause allergies, hyperactivity, liver damage, infertility, anemia, cancer and birth defects [10]. So it is beneficial if we could develop a method to screen the presence of MY in such compound.

Due to high sensitivity, accuracy, and precision of the electrochemical techniques are widely used for the determination of biological and environmental analysis [11, 12]. With the development of more sensitive pulse methods, the electroanalytical studies are more frequently used for the industrial and environmental applications. Most of the electroanalytical techniques are based on the concept of continuously changing the applied potentials to the electrode/solution interface and the resulting measured current [13], therefore the modification of electrode surface by physical or chemical means will play crucial role in such measurement by enhancing the sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility [11–14]. Several approaches have been made to fabricate the modified electrode for the electrochemical detection/analysis of biological and environmental samples [15–17]. In most of the cases carbon based electrodes such as carbon paste, glassy carbon, carbon nano fibers, carbon nanotubes etc. modified with modifiers like chemical compounds, nano materials, ionic liquids or surfactants were used as a working electrode [16–21].

Both MY and CU are electrochemically active, therefore they can be detected electrochemically. Li et al., fabricated graphene modified glassy carbon electrode for the electrochemical detection of curcumin [22]. Electrochemical behavior of curcumin at a platinum electrode was explored by Masek et al. [23]. Later in 2015 Semiha Çakır et al. developed PdNp-graphite electrode by aqueous solution process with Al3+ for electrocatalytic oxidation of curcumin [24]. Recently Zhang et al. reported the electrochemical determination of curcumin by using electrochemically reduced graphene oxide modified glassy carbon electrode [25]. Electro activity of MY on Pt electrode has also been studied and an irreversible cathodic peak due to the reduction of NN was reported by Jain et al. [26]. Different methods like high performance liquid chromatography - electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry [27], high performance capillary electrophoresis [28], micellar chromatographic method [29] were also been used for detection of metanil yellow.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emergent star in the nanocarbon family, due to their benign, abundant and inexpensive nature [30]. CQDs are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with sizes <10 nm, first obtained during purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis in 2004 [31]. CQDs show high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, and high resistance to photo bleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [30, 32–35].

Till now, there hasn't been any report on the electrochemical detection of MY in the presence of CU, therefore we made an attempt to address this issue. In our previous report we explained about the synthesis and characterization of hydroxyl passivated CQDs, which was applied for the fabrication of modified glassy carbon electrode (CQDs/GCE) and used for the electrochemical detection of dopamine [36]. In this work, we extended the application of the same CQDs for the fabrication of CQDs/GCE for the electrochemical detection of MY and CU simultaneously.

Metanil yellow and curcumin were purchased from Merck, Mumbai, India. Demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin were purchased from sigma-aldrich, Milwauke, WI, USA. A fresh solution of CU and MY was prepared by dissolving them in ethanol. 0.1 M Phosphate buffer (5.4 pH) as the supporting electrolyte. All the reagents were extra pure and were used without further purification. The garden fresh turmeric was used for real sample analysis and a MY added turmeric powder as the adulterated sample.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a VSP-potentiostat/galvanostat (Biologic Science Instruments) consisting a conventional three-electrode system with a bare or CQDs modified glassy carbon electrode (3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The glassy carbon electrode was cleaned thoroughly by polishing with successive slurries of 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05 μM alumina in distilled/deionized water on a polishing micro cloth prior to the modification. The electrode was sonicated in distilled/deionized water for 10 min between slurries. Subsequently, 0.5 μL nafion oil (0.5 wt%) was added on to its surface followed by the addition of 7 μL CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) and then air dried for 15 min.

CV in the potential range from −0.6 to 1.3 V was used to study the electrochemical behavior of both MY and CU on BGCE and CQDs/GCE in PBS of pH 5.4.The detection limit and selectivity of the electrode was established by DPV in the potential window of −0.6 to 1.0 V.

The CQDs was prepared from maltose by the procedure reported in our earlier work [36]. Briefly, the charring product obtained by the microwave digestion of maltose was stirred for half an hour at 800 rpm with a much diluted solution of NaOH (100 mL of 8 × 10−5 M) of pH 7.4). For the fabrication of CQDs/GCE, 0.5 μL nafion oil was added on to the cleaned surface of glassy carbon electrode followed by the addition of 7 μL CQDs and then air dried for 15 min.

Electrochemical property of the fabricated electrode was explored by treating potassium ferricyanide/ferrocyanide probe in KCl supporting electrolyte. Fig. 1 showed the cyclic voltammogram of K3Fe(CN)6 in 0.1 M KCl at 50 mV/s on bare and modified electrodes. In case of BGCE (curve a), the oxidation peak was observed at a potential 181 mV with a peak current of 9.1 μA and the reduction potential was obtained at 113 mV with peak current −13.5 μA. The peak to peak separation on BGCE was 68 mV. In comparison with BGCE, the modified electrode illustrated higher oxidation (13 μA) and reduction current (−17.5 μA) with a shift in potential (curve b). The observed peak separation potential of 62 mV, was nearer the value of 59/n mV expected for a reversible system, suggesting the reversible behavior of the redox couple of K3Fe(CN)6 on CQDs/GCE in KCl medium. The increase in peak current and shift in peak potential confirmed the electro catalytic performance of the fabricated electroFsde. The concentration of CQDs and the time taken for the electrode preparation was optimized. It was observed that 7 μL concentrations of CQDs for 15 min were sufficient to produce maximum sensitivity (Fig. S1a & S1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of MY on CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of CQDs/GCE and BGCE in the presence of 2 μM MY in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.4 was investigated by CV at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. As showed in Fig. 2 , at BGCE, two anodic peaks (Epa1 and Epa2) and two cathodic peaks (Epc1 and Epc2) were obtained. The Epa1 and Epa2 were located at a potential of 0.325 V and 0.451 V with a peak current of 4.3 μA and 5.2 μA respectively. Similarly the Epc1 and Epc2were obtained at a potential of 0.403 V and 0.242 V with a peak current of −5 μΑ and −5.7 μΑ respectively. For the CQDs/GCE, both the oxidation and reduction peak currents increased remarkably with shift in their peak potentials. The oxidation peak potentials (Epa1 and Epa2) expressed a negative shift to −0.004 V and 0.136 V respectively. Similarly, a negative shift in Epc1 and Epc2 to potentials 0.112 V and −0.048 was confirmed. These results indicated the high electrocatalytic activity of CQDs/GCE towards the redox reaction of MY in PBS. It is understood that the existence of CQDs is an ideal support material and they promote electrons effectively for electrocatalytic oxidation and reduction process of MY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on the electrocatalytic oxidation of MY at CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of scan rates on the electrocatalytic oxidation of 2 μΜ MY on CQDs/GCE was examined by CV and the results were shown in Fig. S2a. From the figure it is evident that both the values of redox peak currents (Ipa and Ipc) increased linearly with increasing scan rates in the range from 50 to 150 mV/s. Further when the graph of redox peak currents (both Ipa and Ipc) Vs scan rate was plotted (Fig. S2b), all the currents were linearly proportional to the scan rate. These results confirmed that the electrochemical redox reaction of MY at modified electrode was a typical surface adsorption-controlled process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MY concentration and solution pH on CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The set of representative cyclic voltammograms recorded at CQDs/GCE in the PBS supporting electrolyte of pH 5.4 in the presence of MY of increasing concentration was as presented in Fig. 3a. It can be noticed from the CV that all the four redox peaks increases with increase in concentration of MY (from 0.2 μΜ to 400 μΜ). Further the plot of anodic peak currents (Epa1& Epa2) were plotted against log MY concentration (Fig. 3b), showing good linearity with correlation coefficient of 0.994 and 0.996 respectively.

In order to acquire the finest peak response, the cyclic voltammograms of MY on CQDs/GCE were recovered in different buffer solutions like acetate, phosphate and citrate buffer solution. Peaks with different shape and peak potential appeared in these buffers solutions. The results revealed that the peak shape was preeminent and peak current was highest in phosphate buffer solution. Further the pH studies disclosed a maximum peak current for phosphate buffer of pH 5.4. Phosphate buffer solution of pH 5.4 was used as the supporting electrolyte for further analysis.

DPV for 0.1 M MY in PBS of pH 5.4 on CQDs/GCE illustrated two oxidation peaks at a potential of 0.069 V and 0.208 V respectively. A series of DPVs were also recorded at various concentrations of MY (Fig. 4a). Here the second oxidation peak dominated and exhibited additional current than the first. Hence the second oxidation peak current was considered for the calculation of lower detection limit. The graph of oxidation peak current Vs log concentration of MY (Fig. 4b) disclosed that the oxidation peak currents increased with the increase in MY concentration from 0.06 to 40 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. The concentration that gave a signal equal to three times of standard deviation of blank signal was taken as the lower detection limit and was calculated as 0.03 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of CU on CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 demonstrated the cyclic voltammograms of 2 μM curcumin in 0.1 M PBS of pH 5.4 on GCE (curve a) and CQDs/GCE (curve b) with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. On BGCE, CU gave two oxidation peaks i.e., Epa1 at 0.209 V& Epa2 at 0.478 V and a reduction peak (Epc1) at 0.129 V. Under similar experimental conditions on CQDs/GCE, CU exhibited a much larger peak current with negative shift in potentials (0.281 & 0.558 V) and a broad cathodic peak (0.259 V). The relationship between the scan rate and redox peak currents (Fig. S3a) indicated the increasing peak currents with increase in scan rate and a good linearity between the two (Fig. S3b). From the experiment we perceived that the oxidation peak potential became more positive as there was increase in the scan rate. It implied the interaction between the electrode surface and the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution pH and concentration of CU
ce:section.ce:para: Influence of buffer solution pH towards the electrochemical process of CU on CQDs/GCE was analyzed by varying the pH from 4.4 to 9.4. It was evident that the finest peak shape and the maximum peak current was when the buffer solution was of pH 5.4 (Fig. S4a). Oxidation peak current increased and peak potential became more negative, but the difference of peak potential hardly changed as the pH was increased in experiment range. Good linearity between peak potential and pH appeared in Fig. S4b under optimal conditions.

The relationship of peak current with the concentration of CU was evaluated by plotting cyclic voltammogram of varying concentrations of CU in PBS of pH 5.4 (Fig. 6a). All the redox peaks intensified during the addition of CU from 0.8 to 100 μM. From the plot of log concentration of CU Vs oxidation peak currents, it was clear that a linear relationship existed between them (Fig. 6b).

In order to calculate the lower detection limit, DPV of CU in PBS of pH 5.4 on the CQDs/GCE was plotted (Fig. 7a). Here, two oxidation peaks appeared at the potential of 0.288 V and 0.56 V. The first peak produced greater current than the second. Therefore the first peak was considered for the lower detection limit estimated as 0.1 μM. Moreover, a proportional growth in these two peaks was witnessed while increasing the concentration from 0.4 to 200 μM (Fig. 7b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous analysis of MY and CU
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of a mixture of 2 μM MY and 10 μM CU at the BGCE and CQDs/GCE was studied. Fig. S5 showed two oxidation peaks at 0.306 &0.558 V on the BGCE (curve a) for the mixture. However, at the modified electrode (curve b), all the peaks corresponding to the oxidation signals of MY and CU were very clear and the oxidation peak currents of MY and CU increased immensely. Among the four peaks, the first two peaks at −0.004 V and 0.136 V were due to the oxidation of MY and the other two at 0.281 & 0.558 V were due to the CU oxidation. Consequently, the CQDs/GCE could be applied towards the detection of MY in presence of excess amount of CU. The probable mechanism for the separation of MY and CU peaks can be explained as follows: electrostatic repulsion between the MY anions and the negative groups at the modified electrode retarded the electron transfer and shifted the potential of MY towards a more negative value so that the peak of MY could be separated from that of CU. As a result of this negative shift, the presence of CU had no contribution on the anodic current of MY.

The determination of MY and CU was finally performed with the DPV method and the height of the peak was selected as the analytical signal. Fig. S5 (inset) showed the DPV responses of simultaneous oxidation of MY and CU on the CQDs/GCE. The individual electrocatalytic oxidation peaks for MY were at about 0.069 and 0.208 V respectively and for CU at about 0.288 V and 0.56 V, respectively. Overall, the fabricated CQDs/GCE could be successfully used for quantitative determination of MY and CU simultaneously.

In presence of higher concentration of CU (10 μM), the electrochemical oxidation peaks of MY with different concentrations (0.06 to 50 μM) were analyzed and discovered that it was possible to identify the presence of MY in company of higher concentration of CU (Fig. 8a). Similarly, the electrochemical oxidation of CU was also investigated by varying the CU concentration (0.4 to 10 μM) while the MY concentration was constant (5 μM) (Fig. 8b). The peak current increased proportionally with increasing CU concentration and there were no changes in the peak potentials and currents of MY on CQDs/GCE.

A few other common coexisting substances like demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin were examined on the fabricated electrode. When 2 μM MY was determined under the optimum experimental conditions, interferences were hardly observed from the presence of 10 μM of demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin. Even in the presence of demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin the peak current and peak potentials of MY remains unaltered, which means these compounds are not interfering to the oxidation peak currents or peak potentials of MY. The results obtained were summarized in Fig. 9a. Moreover, the influence of the same molecules on the determination of CU was also examined. There were no separate peaks except that the anodic current of CU increased considerably with increasing the concentration of demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin (Fig. 9b). This could be attributed to the fact that demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin underwent oxidation at the same potential where CU oxidation occurred. Their coexistence is considered to interfere with the determination of CU but not to the MY determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MY and CU in turmeric powder samples by CQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The modified electrode was devoted for real sample analysis by preparing two sets of sample system; one of non-adulterated and the other of adulterated samples. For the preparation of adulterated sample, known amount (0.2, 0.6 and 1 μM) of MY was added to the turmeric samples and pure turmeric powder was chosen as non- adulterated sample. The ability of CQDs/GCE to discriminate the turmeric samples with and without MY was analyzed. The electrode could detect the MY present in the adulterated turmeric powder with satisfactory recovery rates. On the modified electrode the second oxidation peak was very predominant than the first and for the higher concentrated MY two peaks were very clear. In case of CU peaks, the peak currents were higher than the expected value. This may be due to the interference from other species like demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin. Subsequently, the non-adulterated samples did not give any peaks corresponding to the MY. All the sets of adulterated samples were analyzed and the results were tabulated in Table 1 . Largely, this method can discriminate between non adulterated and adulterated turmeric powder containing different concentrations of MY.

We successfully developed CQDs/GCE based methodology for the simultaneous determination of CU and MY in food stuff (turmeric powder) using electroanalytical method. The fabricated electrode was characterized by electroanalytical techniques. Individual studies of CU and MY were also carried out by the proposed method. On the fabricated electrode, individual CU and MY gave two peaks each at their oxidation potentials. The DPV and CV technique using the CQDs/GCE showed a good resolution for the oxidation peaks exhibited by MY and CU and a high stability for the electrode performance. The applicability of the proposed method was confirmed by different sets of experiments that yielded reproducible values. A lower detection limit of 0.03 μM and 0.1 μM was estimated for MY and CU with the fabricated electrode. The interference studies revealed that the CU, demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin present in turmeric powder did not interfere in the determination of MY by the modified electrodes. Therefore, the electrode is a promising candidate for the analysis of MY in turmeric powder and MY adulterated food stuffs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ms. Shereema R. M. and Sharath Shankar are thankful to CSIR, New Delhi, India for the award of Senior Research Fellowship and Nehru Postdoctoral Fellowship respectively. Vishnu R acknowledges DST, Govt. of India for Inspire fellowships.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.07.055.
